glossary.def: The memorylessness assumption: the probability of the next state depends only on the current state, not on the full history of how the chain arrived there. It collapses a sequence into a transition matrix.

A row-stochastic matrix P whose entry P_ij is the probability of moving from state i to state j. Every row is a probability distribution summing to 1, and one step of the chain is the multiply π ← πP.
A distribution π left unchanged by the chain, πP = π — the left eigenvector of P for eigenvalue 1. For an ergodic chain it is unique and is the long-run fraction of time spent in each state.

The property of a chain that is irreducible (every state reachable from every other) and aperiodic (not locked into a cycle). Ergodic finite chains converge to a unique stationary distribution from any start, and time-averages equal space-averages.

The reversibility condition π_i P_ij = π_j P_ji for all i, j. It is a stronger, local sufficient condition that implies stationarity, and is the property Metropolis–Hastings engineers to make a chain target any chosen π.

A Markov chain whose states are unobserved; you see only emissions whose distribution depends on the current hidden state. Likelihood (forward), decoding (Viterbi), and learning (Baum–Welch) are solved by dynamic programming in O(K²T).

A family of methods that sample from a target distribution by constructing a Markov chain whose stationary distribution is that target, then running it. It needs the target only up to a normalizing constant, which is why it dominates Bayesian computation.

The canonical MCMC algorithm: propose a move from q, then accept it with probability min(1, [π(θ')q(θ|θ')]/[π(θ)q(θ'|θ)]). The target's unknown normalizer cancels in the ratio; Gibbs sampling is the special case with acceptance always 1.

Rabiner, L. R. (1989). A Tutorial on Hidden Markov Models and Selected Applications in Speech Recognition. Proc. IEEE 77(2).
Geman, S. & Geman, D. (1984). Stochastic Relaxation, Gibbs Distributions, and the Bayesian Restoration of Images. IEEE TPAMI 6(6).
Page, L., Brin, S., Motwani, R. & Winograd, T. (1999). The PageRank Citation Ranking: Bringing Order to the Web. Stanford InfoLab.
Roberts, G. O., Gelman, A. & Gilks, W. R. (1997). Weak Convergence and Optimal Scaling of Random Walk Metropolis Algorithms. Ann. Appl. Probab. 7(1).
Hoffman, M. D. & Gelman, A. (2014). The No-U-Turn Sampler: Adaptively Setting Path Lengths in Hamiltonian Monte Carlo. JMLR 15.
